item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You turned one of my favorite video game characters into a horrifying SCP article. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just want to let you know that this one absolutely horrifies me, because it started off so comical and carefree and then slowly became something out of my nightmares.

Please accept my +1 as I go cry in the fetal position.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tried to think of a more constructive comment, but all I've got is that I'm pretty sure this thing escaped containment and lives in my brain now.
+1 from me, I loved how the writing got better as the creativity was stolen over time. Also really love how spot-on that early bad writing was; gave me flashbacks to my deviantart days..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, in the addendum the junior researcher is referenced as Valeria. i am unsure if it is their first name or a typo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really doubt she lost all emotion, considering she shows frustration at being unable to craft a simple metaphor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's pretty much exactly the comment I was going to make. I think it's far more realistic that she'd just be demoted to janitor or whatever menial task that doesn't require creative problem solving.
The Foundation isn't (unnecessarily) cruel and they certainly aren't stupid so even if the dates were changed around I still wouldn't believe they discovered the secondary effect after the fact.
I imagine they're fairly meticulous in their screening process and they would have a general idea of her abilities at time of hiring so a drop in performance after exposure to her first SCP would've been a huge red flag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, cool, I found a new champion for 'idea I find most terrifying'.

Seriously, I know I'm super late to the party on this one but it's fantastic. The inclusion of a relatively mediocre to poor outside IP sells the fact that this is a literal (and literary) parasite, feeding off of inspiration. This is legitimately the scariest article on the site for me personally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Every time, every time i think i have seen everything on this site.
Oh boy here he goes again.
Bam! Just like that i see anomalous kirby fanfiction and sentient self inserting books.
I mean 432 and 3450 are 2 thing im doing with my own story writing so this was relatable as hell.
It never ceases to amaze you huh?
Whats next? an scp who manifest in forums and if you look around the site you can spot it?
Now that just ridiculous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: don't care that this was 2 years ago you still get my respect

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And he's called Smellsbad McSmellyface

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why not use a complete pacifist character? Who has no power at all, whatsoever, is beyond stupid, a complete and utter coward, has no social skills, smells unpleasant but not bad enough to make people run away, pick his nose, has no control over his anal sphincter, has no creativity, bad oral hygiene and thinks they are the most amazing person ever.

And everything they ever do automatically backfires, and they end up getting hit in the nuts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. My only concern with this one is that I don't think they would dismiss the Researcher that was sucked of creativity. As the first known victim of repeated exposure, she is too valuable as a subject for observation and research herself.

item.title: A Kirby SCP?
item.content:encoded:
*Goes on a big ramble about how canonically Dedede isn't a bad guy anymore*

I'm gonna be honest… dunno how I feel about this one, I mean it's good and I got a good laugh from it, being a big Kirby fan. but something about Dedede (my favourite fictional character BTW.) getting killed gruesomely irks me the wrong way… and i like that. +1 from me!

item.title: A lot of interesting questions
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot of interesting questions here. This makes me think about what would happen if the results of the SCP are tested to their extent. Partially the creativity draining part, but mostly the part about the character being Dedede's ally and fighting and getting eaten by Kirby.

+ show block
– hide block

The character is always an ally of Dedede's and an enemy of Kirby's. What if there's a character whose entire gimmick is they refuse to side with Dedede and are actually Kirby's best friend, or long-lost sibling, or love interest?

They always get sucked up by Kirby. What if there's a character whose entire gimmick is they can't be sucked up? This may have been kind of answered already- apparently there was a similar character in an earlier version of the page, and it was resolved with Meta Knight giving Kirby his sword- but it's still an idea.

What if there's just a character designed to test and break every single part of the fic? He's constantly on fire and incapable of being in bushes without the bush being destroyed in half a second, he's a fierce rival of Dedede's and refuses to be his henchman, he's magically tethered to the ground and incapable of jumping, he's completely indestructibly by any means whatsoever and is also immune to the plot and meta attacks regarding the fictional medium he's in (so Jennifer Harwell could write "he died" and he wouldn't die, and the author wouldn't be able to write him out because he's bored), he's immune to Kirby's inhalation and the effects of wind and breath in general, he's too large to swallow normally without the use of inhalation, everything that happens around him is retroactive from two seconds ago so it's literally impossible for anything to happen suddenly… Something specifically designed to break every trend that 3450 has ever been found to follow, just to see what would happen.

What if a character was made based on ●●|●●●●●|●●|●? If the character sheet was literally just its file? Would 3450 even be able to interpret that? And if it did, how would it write it down? Would it be like "all of a sudden, [poorly drawn picture of ●●|●●●●●|●●|●] jumped out from behind the bushes! Hahaha, said Dedede, you will never be able to defeat my number one henchman, [poorly drawn picture of ●●|●●●●●|●●|●]! [poorly drawn picture of ●●|●●●●●|●●|● shooting a laser beam], but kirby managed to avoided it!" Or would it actually say ●●|●●●●●|●●|●'s SCP number and/or name? And if it did, how would ●●|●●●●●|●●|● respond? Would it manage to take the data itself, or just the flash drive, or even take it at all? And if we consider 3450's anomalous effect with it being able to drain creativity, would this result in ●●|●●●●●|●●|● losing the ability to recognize information regarding itself?

How would Fred interact with 3450? We already know it won't happen- the test was forbidden by 05-█ in Experiment Log 423 A- but what if it happened anyway? Would Dedede's ally be Fred, or would Dedede's ally be Fred's character? If his ally is Fred, if Fred got eaten by Kirby, would he be safe, or would Fred Kirby replace Fred as SCP 423, with the ability to travel through fiction? And if his ally is Fred's character, what kind of character would Fred make?

As someone mentioned above, 682's unkillable nature wouldn't particularly matter in this case since it's a bad fanfic and doesn't really make sense. A character based on 682 (or a character who is literally just 682, based on its file) would likely die anyway, since the fanfic is bad enough to ignore 682's unkillability. So, what would happen if 682 was made a character, and the story was copied down into a physical notebook and used with SCP-826, while 682 was reintroduced to 826? Again, this won't ever happen, since after 682's interaction with 826 and The Generally Nice, Friendly Thing That Can And Will Kill SCP-682 Permanently if it So Much As Spots That Damn Lizard, 682 has refused to enter 826, but what would happen anyway? Which is more powerful, 682's plot armor or 3450's status as a bad fanfiction? And going back to 3450's anomalous effects of sucking creativity, would it be able to suck away 682's ability to adapt in meaningful ways?

There are a lot of interesting questions here, and I'm not sure what the answers would be.

item.title: I love Kirby
item.content:encoded:
Do you know NME Salesman?

I thought DDD used that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was really into this one right up until the final notice at the end, which just seems unnecesarily cruel. The dates indicate that they definitely knew it was the effect of the SCP harming her ability to work, yet the note just basically calls her incompetent and stupid, and acknowledges nothing of how they clearly know the SCP is to blame for her condition. The director claims she deserves an explanation, and then basically says it's all down to her work ethic and level of intellectual competency.

This sort of non-apology really rubs me up the wrong way to be honest.
It's a no-vote from me.

item.title: So, the grammar improvement only affects a single reader?
item.content:encoded: So, the grammar improvement only affects a single reader? No one else's copy gets better grammar?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two months is not too late. Readers still try to influence changes on skips years old.

That said, the secondary effect only became known and noted after her final attempts, which she made two weeks after the Foundation thought she had walked away from the thing. True she started showing symptoms before that, but that might've been written off as non-anomalous frustration/fatigue. Those last two tries really sunk her psych, though, which definitely caught the Foundation's attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's kind of the read I got from it, considering the letter of termination made no mention of the skip or the previously unknown effect it had. It seemed like all they saw was their new employee's work habits slip as she wasted more time and energy on writing fanfiction and essentially "playing with" the skip rather than coming up with new ways to test it. They'd let her go, notice the relation in retrospect, and add an addendum on it's secondary effect after the fact. Except…

On second read...
...I noticed some things

The dating of the addendums on the secondary effect comes two weeksbefore the notice of termination. They totally knew what it did to her and not only did they not mention it to her in the letter, they allowed her to continue to subject herself to the skip without stopping her.

Rivera was a newly-hired junior researcher being given her very first assignment, practically an intern by Foundation standards. Hell, she might have just been told she was being made an official researcher while they shoveled her on to the skip as a test subject. What better way to test the loss of capacity than to watch a promising young mind go to waste? Probably a lot of better ways. The Foundation is cold, but not stupid.

but
When you check the dates...
...the timeline becomes clearer

The addendums on the secondary effect come after her final attempt, meaning the Foundation did not knowingly allow her to continue self-experimentation after the effect had become known. They probably didn't even know she was still working on Larry Plotter in those two weeks leading up to it.

The two weeks after the final experiment might have been time they spent trying to undo the mental damage the way they usually treat cognito-hazard survivors: amnesics. If they couldn't get her brain to reboot and recover, I guess they just let her go.

There is no log of them trying to fix it, which could either be interpreted as the Foundation covering up their failure or that they didn't even try. Adding another addendum with a "credentials lock"-type title could expand on that part and give us either one of those stories and the all the implications to Foundation ethics and practices, but unfortunately it wouldn't be able give us both :(

item.title: My two favorite things...
item.content:encoded:
SCP and Kirby fanfiction? In one package? Sign me the FUCK up. Kirby fanfic was what got me into writing in the first place and that's how I ended up here.

On a more serious note, I really enjoy the concept of bad fanfic slowly draining one's creativity. As an avid consumer of fic, I'm easily weighed down when everything I find is bland and unengaging. The thought of that bland, unengaging content sucking away my creativity? Terrifying. I got into writing in order to add to the fandom so it wasn't all about shipping, or Meta Knight's days in the GSA, or sequels to the anime. If I had run into this scip, I would be an entirely different person today. I've built my life on making up stuff. Hell, a few times it was the only thing left tying me to this mortal coil. What would I be like if I never could?

Anyway, this scip hit a deeply personal spot in me, and the more I think about it, the scarier it gets. You've earned this +1, even if I'm super biased towards the premise.

item.title: Wait... That's Me!
item.content:encoded:
How has it taken me this long to learn this skip existed? Not only is it good, but its got my namesake in it. Like… I get this feeling so much when it comes to writing or academics that you feel like you've got nothing left!

I'll admit too, I'm a little salty I did not write the "Kirby sucks out your creativity" skip. But you did better than I probably could have.

Just take my +1. Take it. Before I suck you up with my breath too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a crappy fanfiction, of course 682 would die.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Normally I like cases where the foundation screws up and someone gets screwed over by a lack of understanding of an SCP, but the secondary effect here just didn't work. They should have known before the researcher got her head messed up. Things like SCP-2310 work well for me, because they put the emphasis on the idea that the Foundation is comprised largely of humans, who make human mistakes. But this one just doesn't work for me. I don't see HOW this mistake got made. The researcher's behavioral changes should have been noted way before. I want to know more about the researcher who should have noticed this, since they messed up big time. Unless they were the researcher on the project? In that case it would make sense, it was their job to monitor themselves. The horror of losing your creativity is good and… Sadly relatable. But I just can't buy the Foundation not noticing someone going through a serious mental decline and not stepping in. I think a note saying that the head researcher was fired or something would do a lot to fix that for me.

I mean this is a two month old skip but I thought I'd get my two cents in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
HAHAHA! you can't steal my originality, BECAUSE I DON'T HAVE ANY ORIGINALITY!

i still wonder what would happen if your OC basically was scp 682 though…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a new avenue of horror. A lot of us have been desensitized to body horror, but what about something we hold more dear to our hearts? Creativity.

+1, looking forwards to your future work

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Foundation personnel embedded within the fan fiction community"
…I doubt many such personnel would exist, honestly. That's a silly idea. [/sarcasm]

One thing I would have liked to see would have been at least one attempt at using disadvantageous copy ability shenanigans (like Sleep Kirby, maybe) before the monotonous uncreativity.

What about an imposing character whose only ability is to weaken themselves? They would approach Kirby menacingly, get sucked up, aaaand… Kirby could only weaken himself and/or look mildly threatening, maybe? Or, would the narrative force this character to defeat himself?

Also, an experiment log involving D-Class subjects could be fun.

Overall, a really cool idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well that was terrifying

I want to say I disagree with the choice to have her fired, and it feels out of character for the Foundation, but at the same time there's something that feels like just a little bit of Kafka in the sheer banality of it.

The abruptness of it almost makes me wonder if the SCP affected the narrative of her life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Woof. This hit hard, especially for a kid that grew up with Kirby, and thought he was the bestest (sic).
I cannot imagine the hell the researcher is going though, becoming a thing made of clockwork instead of a human. She can't be creative. She can't have opinions. She can't feel emotion. She has less function than an AI in the modern age. All because she, and her co-workers were attempting to learn more of something, they thought was, simple. Well done.

item.title: Re: Future Audio Adaptation + Comments
item.content:encoded:
Did I say a week or two? I meant one day. ENJOY!

https://youtu.be/lp_pii7o7Io

item.title: Re: Future Audio Adaptation + Comments
item.content:encoded: Awesome, I appreciate the adaptation! And yeah, that's pretty much what I was going for in the ending (though of course I understand if others see it differently).

item.title: Future Audio Adaptation + Comments
item.content:encoded:
I am pro-final addendum: a lot of people seem to see it as "Rivera being fired for being affected," but in reality, it's more "Well, she is incapable of working for us anymore, and it's kind of our fault because we missed this, so let's get her back in the real world, get her some pay/benefits, and wish her the best." The Foundation can't help her, it's tragical, and they take care of her the best they can.

I am on board with the terror this strikes in my artist's heart; a friend of mine and I are putting together an audio adaptation of this. Should be up in the next week or two, I'll edit this comment to include it when it's done.

item.title: All of a sudden, I up-voted this.
item.content:encoded: The secondary effect is one of the best and most original horror devices I've seen in any media. It's especially effective here, in a community of writers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rest In Peace Junior Researcher Rivera.

You will be missed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I laughed at this comment more than I should have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the one hand, I like the anomaly a lot. I started it skeptical, but the sheer, bloody, stupid inevitability of it was very engrossing. Like, you could write anything, and it would still play out in exactly the same stupid way.

Like some others, though, I'm not ok with the fact that they fired her, especially because they seem to have done it after they had declared a secondary effect (5/11 versus 5/24). More:

+ show block
– hide block

They've suddenly discovered that an SCP they thought they understood fairly well has a new and incredibly damaging effect, and that a researcher is a casuality to it with serious neurological/psychological trauma, and they fire her for it? No psych treatment or investigation in an organization with mandatory regular psych screening that paranoidly checks its employs for subtle memetic influences?

That's not only needlessly cruel, it's an unconscionable waste of a research opportunity. As someone else mentioned, E-Class is a thing. Understanding what actually happened in her head is now just as if not more important to understanding the anomaly than any reader-OC testing. They're going to want to be doing psychiatric testing/treatment and fMRI scans on her around the clock. Exposing D-Class for research purposes after this would be both a waste of human life and a denial of any possibility of recovery for her.

So yeah, I'm not just unhappy with the ending. Unless it was a tragic mistake and they didn't put the pieces together until after they'd fired her, this is just not how the Foundation works. There can be canon differences on that, but firing her is a nearly lolFoundation-level dumb move.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Larry Plotter is my spirit animal.

item.title: Re: not sure
item.content:encoded: Mostly because we don't bother with remembering most of the historical mediocrity. Survivor bias, essentially.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
There is a bit of that, actually. It's subtle, but you can see the spelling and punctuation improve slightly each time. For instance, the quotation marks around Dedede's dialogue don't show up until the last entry.

The effect was a lot more dramatic in my first draft, but it made the early entries a mess to read. It got mixed reviews, so I toned it down.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Like if the writing style got more refined as her characters got blander

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Commander Snivylover: Instead of posting multiple short comments, edit your previous posts using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup, and it's in the rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, I just totally disagree re: the last letter; it works for me so effectively because it's a double-gut-punch of showing just how far the researcher has fallen and showing that the Foundation really can't do anything for them ("Yeah, sorry, you're… not really useful to us anymore. So, moving on…")

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if someone found a way to have their OC team up with Kirby instead of Dedede?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So apparently, Kirby is powerful enough to suck up creativity?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, Relevart, this is absolutely horrifying, and a very easy +1. Great work.

Personally, I think the final addendum firing her is kind of superfluous and lessens the punch of the secondary effect. I'd say just pull it - it doesn't really add anything, and leaving us on Rivera's final note is what you really want us to do anyway.

Either way, this is amazing, and a great first entry. I'm going to go never read Kirby fanfic for the rest of my life now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not just 'writer's block'. Notice at the end…she can't even think of a METAPHOR. A basic analogy or simile.

Spoiler alert
– hide block

This thing isn't just draining creativity, it's draining cognitive function - she'll barely be able to THINK, and everything will be blandly literal.

"What is that, Rivera?"
"That's a sunrise."
"And what is it like?"
"A sunrise."
"How does it make you feel?"
"It's a sunrise."

This is about the most terrifying thing for someone of any kind of intelligence. Just look at people with Alzheimer's - losing your ability to THINK LIKE A HUMAN is just…the worst thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the subtle addition of the excerpts becoming slowly becoming at writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… through my interpretation, basically this is just an SCP who takes your OC's and continues to eat them up until you have infinite writers block? It's a good concept; so, I guess that researchers wouldn't be able to test it themselves or else they'd end up with limited cognitive function or something.

Well, I've upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It slightly stretches my suspension of disbelief that they'd fire someone for being exposed to the effects of an SCP object. Demote (hopefully temporarily) to class-E and admit for treatment, sure, but I can't imagine onboarding a new researcher to the Foundation is easy or cheap, and it doesn't seem quite right for them to respond to one of their staff having her creativity drained by just amnesticising her and tossing her out on the street.
Now, if they didn't actually realize that her loss of creativity was the result of the skip (another stretch, but less of one IMO), this sort of thing would make sense - it just seems dissonant to have "turns out this thing drains your creativity" and "shoo, you're not creative enough" in the same article, in that order.
I also could absolutely see them laying her off and only later realizing that it's the skip that did that to her, and then being unwilling to just drag her back in right after wiping her memory. That could easily be spun as an interesting insight into the Foundation's recruitment practices and ethics, such as they are.

None of this is nearly enough to stop me from upvoting, though. This is a great first article. Great work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, I remember reading in a thread that daveyoufool (the creator of 2337) said that his headcanon for him is that he was created by a portal to a kids television show.

So if you believed that headcanon and the portal was in the Kirby universe… well whoops.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All of a sudden, a woman wearing an SCP uniform jumped out from behind the bushes! Hahaha, said dedede, you will never defeat my number one henchman, Dr. Rivera ! Dr. Rivera took control of this story and used her creativity think of ways to hack it and kill Kirby. Kirby wasn't original enough and got hurt again and again by different warriors and almost died but his powers let him get near and swallow her. After he swallowed her he started to become intelligent and creative and understand anomalies and stuff so he figured out he was in an SCP and used his creativity to learn the rules of the world and hacked them and killed dedede just by willing it. Then he remembered that he was in an SCP read by humans and remembered what Dr. Rivera knew about humans to encòde ͢a̡ waỳ t͞o h̨͘͟a҉c͡k̨ ͜t͢h͘̕e̢͞͞i̵͏r̛ ͢b͠ŗ̷̵a̵͜in̡͝͡s͟͞ i̧͢͡n̨̕͘t͢͟o̢̧͢ ̨͏̀̕̕t͝҉̵͢h͜͏͝e͜͡ ̸͟ś̷̡̛͢t̡̀͘o͢r̴̕y̶ ̨̀h̕͢͢͝e̕͝'̛͡͏s̶̡͘͢͟ ̸̶̀i̴̛͟n̴!̴̢

[WARNING: COGNITOHAZARD DETECTED - REMAIN WHERE YOU ARE]

item.title: Re: How to kill this damn thing. Also, fantastic job!
item.content:encoded: Seconded. Although the SCP itself is well written and with a nice plot twist that you have to think about to understand, the appreciation of the eager researcher failing and getting fired trope really reminds me of some attitudes towards newbies on scp-wiki a few years back. But that's my personal trigger problem, I suppose. The SCP probably does make sense when you use the trope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah but with all this new pataphysics and narrative layer stuff it's not the least ridiculous thing that could possibly happen. Once they try testing this skip with Fred or something it's all over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The main problem with this theory being that, even in-universe, Kirby is not real. The creativity-draining effect is thematically appropriate, but that's about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As many of us know, Kirby is a rather powerful being, able to defeat many terrifying foes. However, Kirby is often rather childlike and simple in mindset.

What if: Kirby is actually far more powerful than we think, with nigh-unlimited shapeshifting and reality-bending power, held in check only by poor imagination and a tendency to copy concepts and ideas off of someone else? (At least once in the show, he became Sword Kirby after sucking up a lightning bolt, showing that his powers aren't cut and dry and need not be limited to exactly what he sucked up.)

Now such a being, having absorbed the creativity and problem-solving aptitude of poor Rivera, is no longer bound, and his true power has been unlocked. Note how terrified the Foundation is of 2006, which they prevent from seeing the outside world. But 2006 can only become what it has seen. Kirby can become anything now. 2006 wants only to spread fear. What does Kirby want? We won't know until it's too late.

Kirby sucked up creativity, and became Creative Kirby. And in becoming Creative Kirby, became a God.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One question on my mind is where is all this creativity going? Is the author leaching people's ideas? Or is it something far more sinister.

item.title: Holy shit.
item.content:encoded:
That secondary effect.

I know that exact feeling, and I'm terrified.

+1

item.title: Re: not sure
item.content:encoded: Double standereds basically, for some reason history is better then modern stuff.

item.title: Re: not sure
item.content:encoded: Why does no one ever say this to scps involving Shakespeare or old shit?

item.title: Re: not sure
item.content:encoded: What's wrong with that?

item.title: not sure
item.content:encoded: I find that although this is well written, I just don't like how it references an outside franchise. Not sure what to think.
